name: Architecture Foundations (Dennemeyer)
description: Microservices boundaries, DDD/CQRS, SOLID and layered/clean architecture, containers and the Kubernetes runtime, and the architect's working practice (design docs, NFRs, testing strategy, safe DB migration, technical leadership).

topics.name: Microservices, DDD & Service Boundaries
topics.description: When services earn their independence: ownership, bounded contexts, CQRS, and Conway's Law.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Microservices means splitting a system into separately deployable services, each owned by a team. The hard question is never "should we use microservices" but WHERE to draw the lines between them.

## What problem does it solve?

In a monolith, many teams share one codebase and one release train — they block each other. Well-drawn services let teams build, deploy and scale independently.

## How it works

A service boundary is justified when it has: clear team ownership, its own API contract, its own data (no other service reaches into its tables), separate scaling needs, and its own monitoring and deploy pipeline. Payment, document generation and notifications make sense as separate services IF those things are true — not because "small is good". Too many services buy you network latency and painful debugging.

Where to find the lines: DDD (Domain-Driven Design) says organize around BUSINESS concepts. Learn the domain language first — trademark, renewal, portfolio — and notice where the meanings shift (a "customer" means different things to billing and to support): those shifts mark bounded contexts, which are natural service boundaries. A Renewal is not a database row; it is a business concept with rules — when it may start, who may trigger it, what states it has.

Conway's Law is the organizational reality check: systems end up mirroring the communication structure of the teams that build them. Badly drawn team boundaries produce badly drawn services — so boundary design is an ownership question, not only a technical one.

CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) sometimes appears at these boundaries: separate models for WRITING (complex business rules) and READING (a fast, denormalized view for dashboards). Use it only where read and write needs genuinely diverge — it doubles your models and introduces eventual consistency between them.

## Common mistakes

- Splitting by team size or fashion instead of domain boundaries
- Services sharing a database — coupled at the data layer, distributed everywhere else
- CQRS by default, paying for two models nobody needed
- Ignoring Conway's Law and drawing boundaries the org cannot own

## Key terms

- Bounded context — where a business term's meaning shifts; a natural boundary
- Ownership — one team, one service, one deploy pipeline
- Conway's Law — the system mirrors the org chart
- CQRS — separate write and read models; not a default
- Modular monolith — the honest alternative when teams are few

topics.name: SOLID, Layered & Clean Architecture
topics.description: Design principles and patterns as means, not ends; layer responsibilities; framework-independent domains.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The design principles (SOLID) and structures (layered and clean architecture) that keep a codebase changeable: small responsibilities, loose coupling, and business logic that does not depend on frameworks.

## What problem does it solve?

Code where everything knows about everything becomes unchangeable: touch one thing, break three others. These principles exist to keep change cheap.

## How it works

SOLID is five principles with one shared goal — small, loosely coupled, testable modules. The most-quoted one is Single Responsibility: a RenewalService should not ALSO generate PDFs, send emails and process payments — those are separate responsibilities that change for different reasons. At the architecture level the same idea applies to whole services.

Layered architecture assigns each layer one job:
- Controller/API: receive the request, validate, call the service. NO business logic here.
- Application/service: orchestrates the use case.
- Domain: the business rules themselves.
- Infrastructure: database, queues, external APIs.

Clean architecture adds the dependency rule: the domain depends on NOTHING external — no framework, no database library, no UI. Infrastructure connects through interfaces (a repository interface the domain defines, implemented by the database layer). Benefit: the business rules are testable without a database and survive framework migrations. Cost: ceremony — for a thin CRUD service it is overkill; for a complex, long-lived domain it pays off.

Design patterns (Strategy, Adapter, Repository...) are named solutions to recurring problems. Use one when it SIMPLIFIES the code — an Adapter converting each partner's format to your internal model is genuinely clearer. Using patterns to look smart adds indirection without value.

## Common mistakes

- Business logic in controllers
- The domain importing the ORM/framework directly — the dependency rule inverted
- Clean-architecture ceremony on a thin CRUD service
- Patterns for pattern's sake — an Adapter wrapping nothing

## Key terms

- Single Responsibility — one reason to change per module
- Layered architecture — controller / service / domain / infrastructure
- Dependency rule — the domain imports nothing from outside
- Repository — an interface hiding data access from the domain
- Pattern as means — apply when it simplifies, not by default

topics.name: Containers & the Kubernetes Runtime
topics.description: Docker/K8s deployment safety, service discovery, gateway vs load balancer, ingress, reverse proxies.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The runtime infrastructure of modern backend systems: Docker packages an app into an image, Kubernetes runs and scales the containers, and a family of components (load balancer, API gateway, ingress, service discovery) routes traffic to them.

## What problem does it solve?

"It works on my machine" and hand-managed servers do not scale to dozens of services. Containers make the app portable and reproducible; Kubernetes automates running, restarting, scaling and rolling out.

## How it works

The working vocabulary: a deployment describes what to run and how many copies; a service gives pods a stable internal address; configmaps and secrets hold configuration; probes check health; rolling updates and rollbacks manage releases.

How traffic finds your code, from outside in:
- Ingress: the cluster's external HTTP routing — api.company.com/renewals → the renewal service.
- Reverse proxy (NGINX or the ingress controller itself): terminates TLS, limits request sizes, compresses.
- API gateway vs load balancer — the distinction interviews ask: a load balancer just DISTRIBUTES traffic across instances and skips unhealthy ones. An API gateway is a POLICY point: authentication, rate limiting, routing, versioning, monitoring. External customer APIs sit behind a gateway; internal traffic usually needs only load balancing.
- Service discovery: pods have changing IPs, so services call each other by NAME (http://payment-service) and Kubernetes routes to the current pods.

What makes a rollout zero-downtime: the new pod starts and its readiness probe stays false until it is truly ready (DB connected, config loaded) — no traffic until then. Old pods get a graceful shutdown: stop taking new requests, finish in-flight ones, close connections, exit. Combined with a rolling update, users never notice the deploy — and a rollback is one command.

## Common mistakes

- No readiness probe — traffic hits pods that aren't ready; deploys drop requests
- SIGKILL-style shutdowns that abort in-flight requests (no graceful drain)
- An API gateway bolted in front of internal traffic that needed only load balancing
- Secrets baked into images

## Key terms

- Image / container — the packaged app and its running instance
- Liveness / readiness probe — "alive" vs "ready for traffic"
- Ingress — external HTTP routing into the cluster
- Gateway = policy, load balancer = distribution
- Service discovery — calling services by name, not IP

topics.name: The Architect's Working Practice
topics.description: Design documents, NFRs first, testing strategy, safe schema migration, technical leadership.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The working habits of the architect/tech-lead role: clarifying requirements before designing, writing short design documents, choosing a testing strategy, migrating databases safely, and leading without managing.

## What problem does it solve?

Most failed projects fail before the first line of code — unclear requirements, undebated designs, unsafe rollouts. These practices front-load the thinking.

## How it works

Non-functional requirements (NFRs) come BEFORE architecture. "We need a renewals API" is not a requirement; daily request volume, latency expectations, audit needs and tolerable downtime are. Availability is a business decision: 99.9% means ~43 minutes of downtime a month; 99.99% is ten times stricter and much more expensive — an internal admin tool does not need what the customer portal needs.

The system design document is short and decision-oriented: goal, scope, requirements, a diagram, the API contract, data model, risks, ALTERNATIVES CONSIDERED, and a rollout plan. Its purpose is to make the design debatable — writing "why async document generation via a queue instead of a sync call" forces the reasoning into the open.

Testing strategy by layer: unit tests for logic, integration tests for DB/API edges, contract tests for API contracts (they catch the backend renaming a field before customers do), E2E for the few critical flows. TDD is a tool for clarifying expected behavior, not a religion.

Safe database migration is a choreography: add the new column (nullable) → the app writes BOTH old and new → backfill existing rows → the app reads the new column → drop the old one later. Every step is backward compatible, so production never breaks mid-migration.

Technical leadership is not people management: clarify requirements, compare options in the open, name risks out loud, slice work into small deliverables, review code, unblock people, and keep product and infra in the loop.

## Common mistakes

- Architecture chosen before traffic/latency/audit requirements are known
- Design docs without alternatives — decisions that cannot be debated
- Schema migrations that break the old app version mid-deploy
- Tech leadership reduced to people management (it is requirements, risks, unblocking)

## Key terms

- NFR — latency/availability/security/cost requirements, gathered first
- Design doc — short, with alternatives and a rollout plan
- Contract test — verifies the API contract both sides rely on
- Expand-and-contract migration — write both, backfill, then retire
- Error budget thinking — availability targets as business choices

questions.title: Drawing service boundaries
questions.prompt: The team wants to split a monolith into microservices. How do you decide where the boundaries go — and when would you say no?
questions.answerStructureHint: Boundary criteria (ownership, data, contract) → domain language / bounded contexts → Conway's Law → costs of too many services.

expectedPoints.label: Boundary criteria
expectedPoints.description: Clean ownership, API contract, data boundary, separate scaling, monitoring + deploy pipeline.

expectedPoints.label: Domain-driven boundaries
expectedPoints.description: Bounded contexts around business concepts; domain language first.

expectedPoints.label: Conway's Law
expectedPoints.description: Team communication shapes the system; boundaries are an org question too.

expectedPoints.label: Names the costs
expectedPoints.description: Too many services → latency, debugging pain; monolith/modular monolith can be right.

followUps.prompt: Where does CQRS fit into this picture, and when would you avoid it?

expectedPoints.label: CQRS judgment
expectedPoints.description: Separate write/read models only when needs genuinely diverge; brings eventual consistency.

questions.title: Where does the business logic live?
questions.prompt: Describe how you layer a backend service and why the domain should not depend on the framework or database.

expectedPoints.label: Layer responsibilities
expectedPoints.description: Controller handles requests only; service/application orchestrates; domain holds rules; infrastructure adapts.
acceptedSignals: no business logic in the controller

expectedPoints.label: Dependency rule
expectedPoints.description: Domain framework/DB/UI-independent; adapters + repository interfaces at the edges.

expectedPoints.label: Principles as means
expectedPoints.description: SOLID = small responsibility + loose coupling + testability; patterns only where they simplify.

questions.title: Gateway, load balancer, ingress — who does what?
questions.prompt: In a Kubernetes deployment, explain the difference between an API gateway, a load balancer, an ingress, and service discovery — and what makes a rollout zero-downtime.

expectedPoints.label: Gateway = policy, LB = distribution
expectedPoints.description: Gateway: auth/rate limit/routing/versioning for external APIs; LB spreads pods and drops unhealthy ones.

expectedPoints.label: Discovery + ingress
expectedPoints.description: Call services by name (payment-service); Ingress routes external HTTP into the cluster.

expectedPoints.label: Zero-downtime mechanics
expectedPoints.description: Readiness gates traffic to new pods; graceful shutdown drains old ones; rollback available.

questions.title: From feature request to design
questions.prompt: Product asks for a new integration feature. Walk me through what you do before any code is written, as the technical lead.
questions.answerStructureHint: NFRs first → short design doc with alternatives → testing strategy → slicing into deliverables + rollout.

expectedPoints.label: NFRs before architecture
expectedPoints.description: Traffic, latency, security, audit, availability clarified first; availability is a business call.

expectedPoints.label: Design doc with alternatives
expectedPoints.description: Goal, scope, contract, data model, risks, alternatives, rollout — understandable and debatable.

expectedPoints.label: Slicing + leadership
expectedPoints.description: Small deliverables (contract, mapping, validation, worker, monitoring); unblock, review, communicate.

expectedPoints.label: Safe DB migration pattern
expectedPoints.description: Add nullable → write both → backfill → read new → retire old.
